title: Arnold Diffusion by Variational Methods, II
speaker: John Mather
affiliation: Princeton University; Institute for Advanced Study

proof_stages.label: Reduction of Arnold diffusion to resonant intervals
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls the announced theorem for perturbed integrable Lagrangians and reduces the construction of orbits visiting prescribed open sets to defining, for each resonant interval gamma, generic sets U_gamma whose intersections define U.

proof_stages.label: Single-resonance averaged Lagrangian and C1-C3
proof_stages.summary: For a resonant interval k1 omega1+k2 omega2+k0=0, the speaker sets up the one-parameter averaged Lagrangian on the quotient torus and states the first genericity conditions: at most two global minima, Morse nondegeneracy, and transverse exchange of two minima.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic selection of strong double resonances
proof_stages.summary: Rational points on the resonant interval are interpreted as double resonances, with a second independent primitive resonance vector, and the c-strong condition imposes a small-denominator bound so that only finitely many double resonances must be controlled.

proof_stages.label: Double-resonance averaged system
proof_stages.summary: At a double resonance the speaker constructs a subtorus T^1_omega, the quotient state space T^2_omega, the averaged potential P_omega, and the kinetic quadratic form K_omega induced from d^2 L0, giving the averaged Lagrangian L_omega=K_omega+P_omega.

proof_stages.label: Revised C4 prime and Jacobi metrics
proof_stages.summary: Using the constant Riemannian metric g_omega determined by K_omega, the Hessian of P_omega at its minimum is diagonalized and the revised condition C4'_omega is imposed, after which the Jacobi metrics G_e=(P_omega+e)g_omega are introduced.

proof_stages.label: Homology class and C5 prime shortest-curve condition
proof_stages.summary: The relevant indivisible homology class h0 in T^2_omega is defined, Morse's simplicity theorem for shortest curves is invoked for e>e0, the failure at the degenerate level e=e0 is identified as the earlier mistake, and the revised C5'_omega condition requires a unique G_{e0}-shortest curve with one of three possible configurations.

examples.name: x-axis picture of rational resonances
examples.note: A rational linear change of coordinates is used to picture the resonant interval as the x-axis, where double resonances are rational points and the c-strong condition is a small-denominator bound; this only makes the arithmetic condition concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker changes coordinates so the resonant interval becomes the x-axis, identifies second resonances with rational points there, interprets c-strongness as a small-denominator condition, and infers finiteness

passes.reason: A rationally transformed resonance line pictured as the x-axis with dense rational points is a standard coordinate model for double resonances, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
The x-axis with rational points is used as an ordinary coordinate model for a rational resonance line and small-denominator resonances, not to refute a claim.
The x-axis/rational-points model is a coordinate-normalized representative of a resonant line used to illustrate rational second resonances and small denominators, not to refute a claim.
A rationally transformed resonant interval to the x-axis with rational points is a standard coordinate-normal form used to illustrate small denominators, not to refute a claim.
An ordinary rational coordinate line whose rational points illustrate second resonances after a change of variables, not a sporadic, limiting, optimal, maximally symmetric, or refuting example.
A rational resonance interval transformed to the x-axis with rational points is an ordinary coordinate model used to illustrate small denominators, not a structurally special object or refutation.
The x-axis/rational-points model is just a coordinate-normalized picture of a resonant interval with dense rational double resonances, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.

The x-axis model is a coordinate-normalized representative of a rational resonant line used to illustrate dense rational/second resonances and small denominators, not to refute a claim, and it has no intrinsic special structure beyond being an ordinary rational line.

The x-axis with rational points is used as a coordinate-normalized illustration of resonances and small denominators, not to refute a claim, and it is structurally an ordinary representative of a resonant line.

Used only as a coordinate-normalized illustration of rational second resonances as rational points on a resonant line, with no adversarial role and no intrinsic special structure beyond being a typical resonant-line model.

A resonant line normalized to the x-axis with rational points is a standard coordinate visualization of dense double resonances, not used to refute a claim and not structurally sporadic, extremal, degenerate, or maximally special among such lines.
The x-axis rational-resonance picture is a coordinate-normalized representative of a rational resonance line with dense rational points, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
